aims: In a modern economy, financial markets and financial Institutions play an extremely relevant role in the allocation of resources. Around the world, recurrent financial crises, such as the 2008 financial crisis, demonstrate the crucial role that financial institutions and financial markets play for economic growth, firm performance and households. Such crises also stress the need to understand these institutions and markets that are commonly misunderstood and misrepresented by the media and policy makers alike. Broadly defined the course has two objectives. First, to understand the role of financial markets and financial intermediaries; their basic characteristics, the challenges and opportunities they face in a market economy. Second, to understand the risks financial intermediaries face and the tools to manage such risks exposures.
course_content: Course Overview and Introduction to Financial Intermediation Key ideas: Preferences and risk; risk-aversion; diversification; riskless arbitrage; market efficiency; asymmetry of information: hidden action (agency costs and moral hazard) and hidden information (adverse selection). Financial Institutions: Economic Role, Regulation and Relevance Key ideas: What is financial intermediation? Why should we care about financial intermediation? (i.e. the special role of financial intermediaries (FIs) in the financial system and the functions they provide); understand why various FIs receive special regulatory attention; understand what makes some FIs more special than others; motivate the basics with some highlights of the 2008 financial crisis Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Depositary and non-depositary institutions; commercial banks, savings institutions, credit unions, finance companies, central banks etc. do? What are the similarities and differences among them? Their role in the financial crisis of 2008? Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Security firms; investment banks; mutual funds; hedge funds; balance sheets–assets and liabilities; Russian Default and LCTM Story Types and Functions of Financial Institutions Key ideas: Insurance companies; life insurance and property-casualty insurance; reisurance; underwriting Types and Financial Risks Key ideas: Interest rate risk; credit risk; liquidity risk; foreign exchange risk; sovereign risk; market risk; off-balance-sheet-risk; operational risk; insolvency risk Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Measuring interest rate risk exposure-repricing model; problems with the repricing model; maturity; term structure of interest rate Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Measuring interest rate risk exposure-duration model; problems with the duration gap model; convexity Measurement of Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Exchange rate risk; interest rate parity; hedging interest rate risk; coverage within and off-balance sheet Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Forward contracts and futures contracts; are they substitutes or they are different; role in hedging interest rate and exchange rate risks Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Options; role in hedging interest rate and exchange rate risks; options vs forwards and futures Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Swaps; interest rate swaps; currency swaps Managing Different Types of Risks Key ideas: Securitization; role in subprime mortgage crisis Course Summary and Overview
learning_outcomes: At the end of this course, students will be able to demonstrate that they understand: The rationale for financial intermediaries, as well as their main functions in a market economy. Identify and evaluate the role symmetric versus asymmetric information plays in the structure and operation of the financial system information. Distinguish the different types of intermediaries and perform financial analysis on them. Understand asset, liability and capital components in the balance sheet of a financial intermediary. Understand how banks earn revenues and profits. Determine the relevant exposures to various types of risks. Understand and use market risk models to manage risk exposure (rates and exchange rate). Understand and use derivative instruments to manage off-Balance Sheet exposures. The basic character of other types of Financial Institutions and roles they play in the economy (including how they are regulated) Technological and other developments impacting the future of Financial Institutions.

aims: This course covers the foundations of finance with an emphasis on applications that are vital for corporate managers. We will discuss many of the major financial decisions made by corporate managers both within the firm and in their interactions with investors. Essential in these decisions is the process of valuation, which will be an important emphasis of the course. Topics include criteria for making investment decisions, valuation of financial assets and liabilities, relationships between risk and return, capital structure choice, the effective use and valuation of derivative securities, as well as, risk management, and real options.
course_content: Time Value of Money, Present and Future Values, NPV Key ideas: financial statements: basics; time value of money net present value (NPV), and annuity formulas Financial Decision-Making: Free Cash Flow Key ideas: NPV ingredients: (a) cash-flows and (b) discount rates Free cash flows (FCFs): earnings are not FCFs Discount rates: (a) time value and (b) compensation for risk Scenario & Sensitivity Analysis; Application Key ideas: Computing FCFs: scenario and sensitivity analysis Annuity formulas and annuity spreadsheet Effective annual rates (EAR), annual percentage rates (APR) Capital Budgeting: Incremental Cash Flows Key ideas: Computing FCFs: incremental cash-flows, timing of CFs, terminal values, sensitivity analysis The link between risk and the cost of capital Capital budgeting: investment decision rules Key ideas: NPV and the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) IRR as bank savings on a real investment. The limitations of using the IRR rule. Project selection with resource constraints Interest Rates and Bond Valuation: Basics Key ideas: Term structure of interest rates, the yield curve Bond basic characteristics: face value, coupons, maturity, STRIPS, yield to maturity, zero-coupon bonds, etc Evolution of bond prices over time Bond Pricing and No-Arbitrage Key ideas: pricing bonds using no-arbitrage; Interest rate sensitivity of bonds Firm Valuation I Key ideas: Equity cost of capital, capital gains, dividend yield Valuation models: (a) dividends, (b) constant growth model (c) total payout model Firm Valuation II Key ideas: (a) Free cash flow valuation models (b) valuation based on comparable firms ("multiples") Valuing Interco Key ideas: different approaches to valuing a firm Assumptions behind alternative valuation approaches Main: (a) discounted cashflow and (b) multiples Risk, the Cost of Capital, Portfolio Choice, Diversification Key ideas: Expected returns, variance and standard deviation Risk-return tradeoff, covariance, correlation across assets Portfolio risk and diversification Describing portfolios and Optimal Efficient Frontier Key ideas: Optimal investment frontier. Optimal portfolios with short-selling. Portfolios with risk-free investments and leverage Optimal portfolios; Sharpe ratios; Minimum required rate of return Market Pricing of Risk Key ideas: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). CAPM: the market is the tangent portfolio. Capital market line: max expected return per unit of volatility. Security market line: market portfolio as benchmark for other investments. Beta of: (1) a security, (2) an asset are not the same. Using betas to compute the expected return of a security Estimating the Cost of Capital in Practice Key ideas: CAPM implementation: risk-free, market index, betas and risk-premia; unlevered betas; equity & debt betas. Estimating betas using historical returns; Using comparables in estimating betas and the cost of capital; formulas to unlever and re-lever betas Valuing a Firm Key ideas: Valuing a firm in practice: (a) project cashflows, and (b) estimate discount rates. Projecting cash-flows: link between forecasts & operations. Estimating the cost of capital: CAPM; Timing of cash-flows and terminal values Financing Investment: Perfect Capital Markets Key ideas: differences between debt and equity. Debt is senior (less risky) relative to equity: a lower cost does not imply that debt is "cheaper." Difference between unlevered and levered equity Weighted average cost of capital (WACC) & project-specific risk Modigliani-Miller propositions: (a) debt does not affect value (b) WACC does not change with leverage Financing Investment: Fallacies and taxes Key ideas: Capital structure fallacies: (a) dilution, (b) earnings per share. Beyond MM propositions. Corporate income taxes & value: interest tax shields. With corporate taxes, leverage affects the WACC and firm value. Who captures the interest tax shields? Financing Investment: Methods to Value the Leverage Effects Key ideas: Main methods to evaluate the effect of leverage on firm value: (a) WACC and (b) Adjusted present value (APV). Explore why firms are not 100 percent debt financed. Extensions to personal taxes Valuation with Leverage: WACC Key ideas: The impact of the costs of bankruptcy and financial distress (FD) on financing decisions; Picking a target capital structure; Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC); Valuing a going concern; practical Issues in valuation Agency Costs of debt and Adjusted Present Value Key ideas: Agency costs of debt: (a) excessive risk-taking, (b) debt overhang. Agency benefits of leverage. Debt can act as a disciplinary device: force managers to payout. Expanded tradeoff theory: taxes, FD, agency and incentives. WACC and APV valuation Leverage: The Role of Information Key ideas: Capital structure decisions as signals of firm quality The role of information in capital structure decisions & firm value Pecking order hypothesis. Implications of information asymmetry for equity issuance. Capital Structure: Summary and Applications Key ideas: Lessons from the recent financial crisis. Discussion on the capital structure of banks, tax policy. Payout policy: dividends and share repurchases Risk Management: When does RM add value? Key ideas: Rationale for risk-management. Hedging as insurance Forward: contract to buy/sell in the future at a price set today Future contracts: forward contract that is traded on an exchange Financial Options I Key ideas: Options: basic characteristics and payoffs. Call (put) options: right but not the obligation to buy (sell) an asset. Using options for risk-management purposes. Options vs. forwards in risk-management Financial Options II Key ideas: Put-call parity Binomial option pricing: call options Financial Options III Key ideas: Binomial option pricing: multi-period binomial option pricing: put options. Black-Scholes formula Determinants of Option Values Key ideas: Using the Black-Scholes formula in practice Black-Scholes calculator in excel. Valuation of employee stock options (ESOs) Challenges of using Black-Scholes formula for valuing ESOs Implied volatility; Determinants of option prices Dynamic Decision-Making and Real Options Key ideas: Real options: flexible investment projects are like financial options: (a) option to wait, (b) option to expand, (c) option to delay. Implications for investment in practice. Compare a DCF valuation to a real options valuation The value of staging investments Key ideas: the value of staging investments in the pharma and venture capital industries: (a) set milestones in advance (b) postpone substantial investments until information arrives (c) drop projects that do not meet the milestones Corporate Securities as Options and Credit Risk Key ideas: equity can be interpreted as a call option on firm value Equity holders right to default is like a put option on the firm's assets Using Black-Scholes to compute the value of debt and equity Course Overview
assessment_methods: Your course grade will be determined by your class participation and in-class quizzes (15 %), performance on each of the two midterm exams (30 %), the final exam (40-55 %), and completion of recommended homework assignments available through MyFinanceLab (0-15 %). The purpose of the weekly MyFinanceLab homework is to keep you up to date with the course material. The amount you complete correctly will determine the weight put on homework in your final grade, up to a maximum of 15 %. The remaining weight will be put on the final exam. (For example, if 1500 total homework points are possible, and you complete correctly 1000, homework will count for 10 % of your grade, and the final will count for 45 %). Because students have unlimited attempts to get assignments right (before each assignment is due), almost all students receive full points on the homework. This means that not doing an assignment puts you at a considerable disadvantage relative to others when the final grades are computed.

aims: This is an advanced undergraduate course in finance. The course focuses on real world market risk models for equity stocks and equity options. Working on real life models, students develop their intuition in finance matters and learn derivatives pricing, risk management and market model implementation. This course prepares students to work in Risk Management for any financial institution.

Volatility Modeling using daily data (Ch. 3 with special emphasis on Section 4 & Ch. 4) 4. Volatility Modeling using intraday data (Ch. 5) 5. Nonnormal distributions(Ch. 6) 6. Correlation Modeling (Ch. 7) 7. Term structure of risk (Ch. 8) 8. Copulas (Ch. 9) 9. Option Pricing (Ch. 10) 10. Option Risk Management (Ch. 11) 11. Credit Risk Management (Ch. 12)

Read and write financial problems (from the formula on paper to Excel) 3. Problem solver and problem implementation: Learn advanced finance concepts and how to solve them in Excel 4. Become an expert of the equity market and its risk management tools

aims: This course is designed to be thorough introduction to derivatives products and markets. We will focus on describing the mechanics, pricing, hedging and applications of the basic linear (forwards, futures and swaps) and non-linear derivatives (options). This class will emphasize the relationship between non arbitrage and risk neutral valuation as the main cornerstone for derivative pricing. On a practical note, we will focus on the use of these instruments to transfer, mitigate, and eliminate different types on financial risk arising in industry situations. We will start by introducing the primary concepts in an intuitive manner and then, after formalizing these concepts in a more rigorous theoretical framework, we will follow with more complex examples and practical applications. We will also emphasize economic intuition, industry relevance and corporate applications whenever possible, especially in the second part of the course.
course_content: 1- Introduction: Definitions. Main types of Derivatives: Forwards, Futures and Options. Exchanges and OTC markets. Hedgers vs Speculators. The No Arbitrage principle. Margin trading and short-selling. 2- Forwards and Futures: Forward pricing using the non-arbitrage principle. Currency Forwards. Commodity Forwards. Lease Rate and Convenience Yield. Futures and its difference with Forwards. 3- Introduction to Risk Management: Hedging with Forwards and Futures. Pros and Cons of Hedging. Basis Risk. Minimum Variance Hedge. Options as Insurance. 4- Introduction to Fixed Income: Bond Basics. Spot/Zero and Forward Rates. Forward Rate Agreements. Eurodollar and Treasury Bond Futures. Duration and Convexity 5- Swaps: Interest Rate Swaps (IRS). Mechanics and uses. Valuation of Interest Rate Swaps. Currency Swaps. Total Return Swaps 6- Introduction to Options: Types of Options. Payouts and mechanics. Put Call Parity. Upper and Lower Bound on Option Prices. Basic option strategies (spreads, collars, straddles, strangles). Applications to Risk Management. 7- Introduction to Option Pricing: Basic replication argument. Two period binomial tree. Risk Neutral Probability. Multiperiod extensions and application to puts and American options 8- The Black Scholes Formula: Binomial Tree and Lognormality. Modeling Stock Returns. Basic Derivation of the Black Scholes Formula. Application to options on dividend paying assets and currency options. 9- Black Scholes Greeks: Delta, gamma, theta, vega and rho. Market making and delta hedging. Option Elasticity. 10- Implied Volatility: Volatility smile and volatility skew. Volatility Modeling and Estimation, ARCH and GARCH 11- Montecarlo Simulation: Simulating random walks and log normal stock prices. Montecarlo valuation of European and path dependent options. Introduction to Jumps. Estimation errors and Variance Reduction Techniques 12- Asset Pricing Theory: Non Arbitrage Revisited. Non Arbitrage, the Portfolio Choice Problem and Risk Neutral Valuation. Martingale Pricing. Revisiting the Black Scholes Formula using Risk Neutral Valuation 13- Corporate Applications Part I: Introduction to Real Options. Investment and NPV Rule. Investing as an Option. Practical Applications:. Investment or abandonment, R&D as an Option, Commodity Extraction. 14- Corporate Applications Part II: Debt and Equity as Options. Debt, credit risk, expected return and yield to maturity. Warrants and Convertibles Bonds. Stock Options. Modeling acquisitions with options. 15- Introduction to Credit Derivatives: Merton’s structural model. Default Probabilities, Exposure and Recovery. Survival, probabilities and hazard rates. Credit default swaps (CDS), mechanics and pricing.
learning_outcomes: By the end of the class, you should at least have a basic understanding of the workings and pricing of the main derivatives instruments and understand their usefulness and applications in a variety of industry settings. In addition, the class should provide a solid theoretical background for those who wish to take more advanced courses in derivatives, asset pricing, or other related topics. More specifically, you should be able to: Describe the mechanics, payouts, and applications of the most common derivatives instruments: forwards, futures, swaps and options in its many variations and forms. Apply non arbitrage arguments and risk neutral valuation to price these derivatives instruments across asset classes (equity, currencies, commodities, interest rate products, etc.). Develop some familiarity with numerical methods, primarily binomial trees and Montecarlo simulation, that can be used to value more complex derivatives for which an analytical solution might not exists. Recognize and understand the main corporate applications of derivatives products, such as real options, warrants and convertible debt issuance.
assessment_methods: The mid-term exam will be a 2.5-3 hour in-class exam, most likely a combination of a multiple choice section and a written section. You will be allowed to bring any summary sheet, class notes or academic reference. Any computing equipment can be used. The final exam will be a 3 hour exam on a date designated by the University. It will be on a similar format to the midterm (open book, part multiple choice, part written section). In addition, I will assign homework after each class. The homework won’t be graded but it is a critical part of the learning process as it serves a twofold purpose: it is meant to consolidate your understanding of the concepts imparted in class via additional practical exercises, and it will help you prepare for the midterm and final exams. The homework questions have a similar style to those on the exam, so I would strongly encourage you to solve all the exercises assigned in due course. Between class attendance, readings and homework assignments, I expect you to devote at least 10-12 hours a week to learn the class material. The grading will be based on the following formula: max (40 % midterm+60 % final, 50 % midterm+50 % final)

aims: The objective of this course is to undertake a rigorous study of various fixed income securities, including their derivatives and study how monetary policy affects the bond market. After a brief overview of the market for fixed income securities we will discuss basic pricing of various types of bonds, interest rate risk management of bonds and their portfolios. We will also analyze the green, social and sustainability fixed income market. Furthermore, we will encounter various types of interest rate derivatives and discuss their pricing. Additionally will also analyze the role of the central bank and inflation when pricing bonds.

course_title: Finance and the Mexican Financial System
aims: The course provides a comprehensive introduction to the fundamental quantitative methods essential in the field of finance, with a specific focus on the Mexican Financial System. The primary goal of this course is to help students gain a deep understanding of financial markets and the various securities traded within them. The course maintains a rigorous approach, expecting students to not only grasp but also apply quantitative methods. In recognition of the constantly evolving composition of financial instruments and markets, this course is designed to adapt to these changes. Real-world examples will be used extensively to illustrate the practical applications of the theoretical concepts. The course provides a foundational understanding of the significance of corporate finance and the role it plays within the Mexican financial system. The course is divided into two parts to facilitate learning and comprehension. The first part of the course will focus on acquiring the knowledge that supports the financial theory, highlighting the importance of the concept of the Value of Money in Time. The second part of the course will focus on the role that financial markets and financial institutions play in a modern economy, their characteristics as well as the challenges and opportunities faced in the distribution of resources in the economy. By the course's conclusion, students will possess a robust foundation in corporate finance and an enhanced understanding of the Mexican financial landscape.
course_content: I. Introduction: The Corporation and Financial Markets a) The Four Types of Firms b) Ownership Versus Control of Corporations c) Primary and Secondary Stock Markets II. The Time Value of Money a) The Timeline b) Valuing a Stream of Cash Flows c) Perpetuities and Annuities d) Interest Rate Quote and Adjustments III. Investment Decisions and Capital Budgeting a) Net Present Value b) Internal Rate of Return c) The Payback Rule d) Profitability Index IV. Mexican Financial Institutions, Financial Intermediaries, and Asset Management Firms a) Overview of the Mexican financial system b) Financial Regulatory Framework in Mexico c) Mexican Central Bank (Banco de México) d) Commercial Banks in Mexico V. Bond Valuation and Mexican Government and Corporate Bonds a) Bond Cash Flows, Price, and Yields b) Dynamic Behavior of Bond Price c) Corporate Bonds d) Current Mexican Government debt instruments VI. Mexican Stock Exchange a) Introduction to the Mexican Stock Exchange (BMV and BIVA) b) Market Participants and Regulatory Framework c) Major stock market indices in Mexico (IPC, FTSE BIVA) d) The Dividend-Discount Model
learning_outcomes: By the course's conclusion, students will possess a robust foundation in corporate finance and an enhanced understanding of the Mexican financial landscape.
literature: Berk, J., DeMarzo. 2020. Corporate Finance. Fifth Edition. Global Edition. Pearson. Websites: https://www.bmv.com.mx https://www.banxico.org.mx https://www.gob.mx/cnbv http://www.mexder.com.mx http://www.biva.mx

aims: The course is based on the premise that the generation of value to consumers, through the satisfaction of their needs and desires, is instrumental in the creation of value for a firm. We examine the role of marketing in the creation of this value, learning concepts that help us understand markets and identify opportunities, and learning to create marketing plans that arise from the evaluation, design and implementation of the appropriate marketing mix. MARKETING-1 is the first of 3 core Marketing courses that students will take for the undergrad program to learn the basic concepts of Marketing. In this course students will learn: The importance of Marketing in current organizations. The environment in which organizations are immersed and how this influences the plan development process. Concepts and steps involved in the development of a Marketing Plan through which organizations define the position and future perspective of the brands. The influence that marketing has for the prevalence and success of organizations. How to create / develop a Marketing Plan to overcome obstacles and align organizational resources towards future success.
course_content: Chapter 1: Marketing: Creating Customer Value and Engagement Dove case Chapter 2: Company and Marketing Strategy Homework: Marketing Myopia Decide which groups presents what topics in Top 10 Global Trends, and presentation order. Chapter 18: Creating Competitive Advantage Chapter 3: Analyzing the Marketing Environment Team Presentations: Top 10 Global Trends (new trends) Chapter 4: Managing Marketing Information to Gain Customer Insights Harvard Case: TruEarth Healthy Foods (Objective: Market research and forecast demand for a new product) Stage 1 GROUP PROJECT PRESENTATIONS Chapter 5: Consumer Markets and Buyer Behavior Homework: Consumer Decision Journey Chapter 7: Customer Value-Driven Marketing Strategy (Segmentation and Target Market) Value of the market and Target Market (Numerical Exercise) Chapter 7: Customer Value-Driven Marketing Strategy (Differentiation and Positioning) Positioning Maps and Statements Stage 2 GROUP PROJECT PRESENTATIONS Chapter 8: Products, Services and Brands: Building Customer Value (Products and Services) Chapter 8: Products, Services and Brands: Building Customer Value (Building Customer Value) Chapter 9: Developing New Products and Managing the Product Life Cycle Chapter 10: Pricing: Understanding and Capturing Customer Value Chapter 11: Pricing Strategies: Additional Considerations Harvard Case: Culinarean Cookware (Objective: Evaluate the pros and cons of price promotions) Chapter 12: Marketing Channels: Delivering Customer Value Chapter 13: Retailing and Wholesaling Stage 3 GROUP PROJECT PRESENTATIONS Chapter 14: Engaging Customers and Communicating Customer Value Chapter 15: Advertising and Public Relations Chapter 16: Digital Marketing Decide order of presentations for Final Projects Chapter 20: Sustainable Marketing: Social Responsibility and Ethics Practice: Profit and Loss analysis (strategies) for Pricing in the Marketing Channel Final GROUP PROJECT PRESENTATIONS Course Review
learning_outcomes: Define marketing and outline the steps in the marketing process. Identify the key elements of a customer value-driven marketing strategy. Understand the company-wide strategic planning process. Design business portfolios and develop growth strategies. Describe a customer value-driven marketing strategy and mix. Describe the environmental forces that affect the company’s ability to serve its customers. Understand how companies can react to the marketing environment. Explain the importance of information in gaining insights about the marketplace and customers. Define the marketing information system. Outline the role of marketing research. Identify the steps in the marketing research process. Define the consumer market and construct a simple model of consumer buyer behavior. Define the major types of buying decision behavior and the stages in the buyer decision process. Define the major steps in designing a customer-driven marketing strategy. Identify the major bases for segmenting consumer and business markets. Explain how companies identify attractive market segments and choose a market-targeting strategy. How companies differentiate and position their products for maximum competitive advantage. Establish a brand differentiation and positioning strategy. Define the brand positioning statement. Define product and describe the major classifications of products and services. Define individual products and services, product lines, and product mixes. Identify the characteristics that affect the marketing of services. Understand branding strategy—brand equity. Identify decisions companies make in building and managing their brands. Understand how companies find and develop new product ideas. Identify and define the steps in the new product development process. Identify the stages and management of the product life cycle. Define price and identify the three major pricing strategies. Understand the importance of pricing in today’s fast-changing environment. Identify and define external and internal factors affecting a firm’s pricing decisions. Describe the major strategies for pricing new products. Explain how companies find a set of prices that maximize profits from the total product mix. Discuss how companies adjust their prices to take into account different types of customers and situations. Identify the major channel alternatives open to a company. Understand how companies select, motivate, and evaluate channel members. Discuss the nature and importance of marketing logistics and integrated supply chain management. Understand the role of retailers in the distribution channel and describe the major types of retailers. Discuss how retailers are using omni-channel retailing to meet the cross-channel shopping behavior of today’s digitally connected consumers. Identify major retailer marketing decisions. Understand the changing communications landscape and the need for integrated marketing communications. Outline the communication process and the steps in developing effective marketing communications. Identify the methods for setting the promotion budget and factors that affect the design of the promotion mix. Define the role of advertising in the promotion mix. Describe the decisions involved in developing an advertising program. Define the role of public relations in the promotion mix. Identify the major forms of digital marketing. Understand how companies respond to the digital age with various online marketing strategies. Learn how companies use social media and mobile marketing to engage consumers and create brand community. Understand competitors and customers through competitor analysis. Identify the fundamentals of competitive marketing strategies. Balance customer and competitor orientations to become a market-centered organization. Define sustainable marketing and discuss its importance. Describe the principles of sustainable marketing. Explain the role of ethics in marketing.
assessment_methods: Midterm Exam 35 % Final Exam 35 % Marketing Plan (Group Project) 20 % Individual Homework 10 % The class will consist of lectures and discussions of marketing theory and practice, Harvard cases, and developing a marketing plan in groups. For the final project, each group will develop a marketing plan for a new product or service. This marketing plan will include an environmental anaylisis, designing and creating the new product concept, research to identify consumer preferences and potential demand, and identifying a strategy to commercialize the product or service to the consumer. This marketing plan will be evaluated based on its creativity, information quality, practicality, group teamwork and the integration of relevant class material. Groups will be formed at the beginning of the semester, and, once formed, may not be changed.
literature: Kotler, Philip and Gary Armstrong, Principles of Marketing. 18th edition, Pearson

aims: This is a first part of a two-semester sequence of microeconomic theory for students specializing in political economy and/or financial economics. The principle object of study in this course is the behavior of rational economic agents, facing given economic environment. The material of this class will form the basis further study of microeconomics, as well as for understanding modern foundations of other branches of economic analysis. The course starts with the analysis of standard theory of choice and review of game theoretic notions such as Nash equilibrium in simultaneous moves games. Both the theory of choice and game theory will be very useful tools that we will use in the further topics of object allocation problems. Significant part of this course is on matching theory, market design and auction theory. We will analyze theoretical models of one-sided and two-sided matching. Topics include stable matching, on-campus housing, school choice, kidney exchange and matching with contracts. In these real-life situations we design specific market clearinghouses to obtain desirable allocations. Later we will learn about first and second price auctions and multi-item auctions. Finally we will analyze the relationship between matching problems and auction problems.
course_content: Preference Relations and Choice Theory Review of Game Theory Object Allocation Problems Matching Theory Auction Theory
assessment_methods: There will be two midterm exams and a final exam in this class. The final exam will be comprehensive. No make-up exam will be given. The final grade will be determined as follows: 25 %-each midterm exam 40 %-final exam 10 %-problem sets

Norton &Company “Two-Sided Matching: A Study in Game-Theoretic Modeling and Analysis” by Alvin Roth and Marilda Sotomayor, Econometric Society Monographs “Lecture Notes in Microeconomic Theory” by Ariel Rubinstein, available online. “Matching, Allocation and Exchange of Discrete Resources” by Tayfun Sonmez and Utku Unver, Handbook of Social Economics, available online.

aims: The main objective in this course is to study the diverse macroeconomic models of equilibrium which contemplate the interaction of various agents. The course will emphasize the applications of these models rather than the theory behind them. It will be assumed that the student knows the individual election models of consumers and producers. General Equilibrium models will be studied in the first part of the course. These models are based on the assumptions of perfect competition. They analyze the implications of consumer and producer interaction in several industries simultaneously. These models have great implications in dynamic macroeconomics, international commerce public finance and private finance. In the second part of the course we will study models characterized by a small number of economic agents in which perfect competition does not apply. In these models, individuals recognize that their decisions are determining an equilibrium and therefore they have a strategic value. In order to be able to understand these models, some game theory studies will be needed. Nevertheless, the study of this theory is not the main objective in this course. An introduction to the subject will be given, with the final objective of exclusively covering that information which will be useful for this course. The course will include asymmetric information models. These are based on the assumption that each agent has different information.
course_content: I. GENERAL EQUILIBRIUM 1.1 Pure exchange model 1.2 2x2 production model 1.3 Production and consumption a) Representative agent b) General model 1.4 Intertemporal equilibrium 1.5 Financial markets II. Game theory 1.1 Introduction to strategic analysis 1.2 Normal static games 1.3 Dynamic games 1.4 Asymmetric information games 1.5 Mechanism design. Principle and agent
assessment_methods: Detailed study of the recommended bibliography. Homework 10 % Participation 30 % Final Exam 60 %

aims: This course will study how firms compete with each other in imperfectly competitive markets. We will start with a brief review of the basic concepts from consumer and producer theory. The remainder of the course will focus on firms’ price and non-price strategies in the case of oligopoly. Examples of the topics covered include price discrimination, collusion, horizontal mergers, predatory pricing, vertical restraints, product differentiation, research and development, and network effects.
course_content: 1. Recap of consumer and producer theory 2. Price discrimination 3. Oligopoly: Bertrand and Cournot models 4. Collusion 5. Horizontal and vertical product differentiation 6. Market structure 7. Horizontal mergers 8. Entry deterrence 9. Vertical integration and vertical restraints 10. Research and development 11. Network models
learning_outcomes: understand the standard economic models for the analysis of imperfectly competitive markets; apply these models to explain the real-world behavior of firms in the Mexican economy and elsewhere; and find relevant questions and data for writing an undergraduate thesis or tesina in the field of industrial organization.

The final exam will be cumulative and based on all material from throughout the course. In that case, the share of the final exam in your overall grade will be 40 %.

aims: This is the first part of a three-semester sequence of microeconomic analysis for students specializing in economic theory. The object of study in this course is the behavior of rational economic agents (consumers and producers), facing a given economic environment. The material of this class will form the basis for the study of, among others, such issues as the general equilibrium resulting from simultaneous actions by multiple economic agents, and strategic interactions between economic agents. All of these will be explored in the later parts of the microeconomic theory sequence. The study of individual choice also forms the foundation for the modern approach to study of macroeconomic phenomena. The formal subject matter of this course is similar to that of the Intermediate Microeconomics classes you may have taken in the past. This class, however, will provide a substantially more formal presentation of the material. Furthermore, a major goal of this class is to equip students with the technical "machinery" required for understanding and conducting modern research in economics. It should, however, be stressed, that this is not a course in applied mathematics, and the mathematics involved is not an end in itself, but rather an important tool required to understand and model complex economic phenomena. It is thus essential, that students try to see the economic intuition behind the mathematical language. The formal mathematical prerequisites for the class are limited to the standard topics in set theory, calculus and probability, which should be familiar to most of the students. If, on occasion, additional material is needed, it will be fully developed in class. Prior training in mathematical analysis is NOT required; the few analysis concepts used in this class (mostly, open/closed sets and continuity) should be familiar to everyone from their calculus courses.

Reny: Advanced Microeconomic Theory, 3rd edition, Addison Wesley, 2011 Kreps, D., Notes on the Theory of Choice, Westview Press 1988 Kreps, D., Microeconomic Foundations I: Choice and Competitive Markets, Princeton University Press 2012 Kreps, D.: A Course in Microeconomic Theory, Princeton University Press, 1990 Varian, H.: Microeconomic Analysis, W.W. Norton Co., 1992

course_content: The course consists of Part 1 and Part 2. Part 1 consists of lectures on the classical general equilibrium theory. Part 2 is the discussion of the papers beyond the classical general equilibrium theory. Part 1 This part covers the basics of the pure exchange economy: Walrasian equilibrium, the first and the second welfare theorems, equilibrium existence, uniqueness, stability (with elements of the genericity analysis), comparative statics, the time and uncertainty interpretation, the core convergence, a simple financial model with incomplete markets (Mas-Colell et al., 1995, Chapters 15-19), markets with a continuum of agents (Aumann, 1964), and comparative advantage (Costinot, 2009). Part 2 The relevant papers will be introduced as the course progresses. The Law of Large Numbers for a continuum of agents (Judd, 1985; Uhlig, 1996). Two-sided matching (Azevedo and Leshno, 2016).
literature: Aumann, Robert J., “Markets with a Continuum of Traders,” Econometrica, 1964, 32 (1-2), 39–50. Azevedo, Eduardo M. and Jacob D. Leshno, “A Supply and Demand Framework for Two-Sided Matching Markets,” Journal of Political Economy, 2016, 124 (5), 1235–1268. Costinot, Arnaud, “An Elementary Theory of Comparative Advantage,” Econometrica, 2009, 77 (4), 1165–1192. Judd, Kenneth L., “The Law of Large Numbers with a Continuum of IID Random Variables,” Journal of Economic Theory, 1985, 35, 19–25. Mas-Colell, Andreu, Michael D. Whinston, and Jerry R. Green, Microeconomic Theory, Oxford University Press, 1995. Uhlig, Harald, “A Law of Large Numbers for Large Economies,” Journal of Economic Theory, 1996, 8, 41–50.

course_title: The Politics of International Economic Relations
aims: The aim of this course is to acquaint students with the foundational theories of international political economy (IPE) and to apply these to the study of some of the principal functions and issues in IPE. In this regard, a core aspect of the course will be to study the relations between political and economic processes and institutions, and notably the relationship between states and markets, in both their domestic and international dimensions. Accordingly, some of the key themes of the course include: What is the market and how well does it function? Do politics come before economics or vice-versa? Does the state remain the most important actor in IPE or is it being superseded by other forces and entities? What implications does globalization hold for governments and governance? How can states and non-state actors work together to resolve collective action problems? The course is comprised of roughly two equal parts. In Part I, students will be introduced to the principal ideas and theorists who have shaped the main approaches to understanding the workings of the international political economy, from Adam Smith down to the present. In Part II of the course, we shall build on this theoretical exposition by applying these conceptual approaches to studying the evolution of the global political economy and analyzing salient issues in contemporary IPE.
course_content: Week I – Introduction to IPE January 17: Course Overview January 19: Defining the Subject Weeks II: Classical Liberalism and Liberal Interventionism January 24: Adam Smith—The Invisible Hand and the Logic of Infinite Accumulation January 26: Keynes—The Instability of Capitalism and the Need for Government Intervention Weeks III-IV: The Tension between States and Markets January 31: Documentary Film—The Commanding Heights (Part I) February 2: Hayek—The Critique of the State as the Arbiter of Social Outcomes February 9: Polanyi—The Critique of the Market as the Arbiter of Social Outcomes Weeks IV-V: Realist Theories February 14: Mercantilism and Economic Nationalism February 16: Hegemonic Stability Theory Weeks V-VI: Structural Theories of IPE February 21: Marx—The Surplus Labor Theory of Value and the Crisis of Capitalism February 23: Internationalizing Marx and the Historical Structuralist Perspective ISSUE AREAS Week VI – VIII: Monetary Relations February 28: History of the International Monetary System: The Emergence of the Global Financial System and the Classical Gold Standard March 2: The Interwar Monetary System and Bretton Woods March 7: The Collapse of Bretton Woods and the Movement towards Monetary Integration Weeks VIII - X: Debt & Financial Crisis March 9: From the End of Bretton Woods to the 1980’s Debt Crisis and the 1990’s Asian Crisis March 14: Documentary Film—“Inside Job” March 16: Financial Crisis in the New Century I—The 2008 Financial Meltdown March 21: Financial Crisis in the New Century II—The European Sovereign Debt Crisis Weeks XI – XII: Trade Relations March 23: Theoretical Foundations of the Political Economy of Free Trade March 28: The World Trading System from 1840-1947 March 30: The World Trading System from 1947 to 1994 and the Case of NAFTA April 4: The World Trading System from 1994 to the present Weeks XIII – XIV: Globalization & Multi-National Corporations April 6: Globalization Defined and Debated April 18: Documentary Film—“The Corporation” April 20: MNC’s in a Globalized World Week XIV and XV: International Development April 25: Strategies of Development April 27: Trade and Development May 2: The Debate over Foreign Aid and Development Weeks XVI- XVII: Energy Markets and the Environment May 4: Documentary Film—The Commanding Heights (Part II) May 11: The International Energy System May 16: The Political Economy of the Environment Week XVII: Conclusion May 18: Whereto from Here? The Future of the Global Political Economy
assessment_methods: There will be a five-page double-spaced take home essay assigned at the end of the “Theories” section (due on Thursday, March 2). Additionally, students must submit two three-page double-spaced review comments on the readings from two of the “issue area” topics. These should summarize and analyze the supplementary readings (i.e. excluding the chapters from the Balaam and Veseth or Cohn texts) which have been assigned for each session. These review papers are due by the start of the session in which the topic is to be covered. All late assignments will be marked down by a full grade point (i.e. 8 to 7) per day of lateness, unless this is cleared with me first. All papers are to be written in English. Finally, a sit-down final examination will be administered during the exam period. Students must pass this exam in order to pass the course. Final grades will be determined according to the following formula: Mid-term Essay – 25 % Review Comments – 30 % (15 % each) Final Exam – 35 % Participation – 10 %
literature: Theodore H. Cohn, Global Political Economy: Theory and Practice, 5th edition, New York, Longman, 2010. Robert Heilbroner, The Worldly Philosophers: The Lives, Times, and Ideas of the Great Economic Thinkers. 6th ed. New York: Simon & Schuster, 1992. David Balaam and Michael Veseth, Introduction to International Political Economy. 4th ed. Upper Saddle River, N.J.: Pearson, 2008. Daniel Yergin and Joseph Stanislaw, The Commanding Heights: The Battle for the World Economy (New York: Touchstone, 2002). Joan Spero and Jeffrey Hart, The Politics of International Economic Relations, sixth edition New York: St. Martin’s Press, inc., 2002. Jeffry A. Frieden and David A. Lake, eds. International Political Economy, fourth edition (New York: St. Martin’s Press, inc., 2000). Richard Stubbs and Geoffrey Underhill, eds. Political Economy and the Changing Global Order, second edition (Don Mills: Oxford University Press, 2000). Robert Gilpin, Global Political Economy: Understanding the International Economic Order, (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2001). Susan Strange, States and Markets (London: Pinter, 1997).

aims: Principles and training required to develop mathematical models to support decision making in different Engineering linear problems. The theory and solution methods related to linear programming problems, including integer, binary, mixed and dynamic programming applications. The use of computer packages in order to solve linear programming problems.
course_content: Linear programming problems and the Simplex Method. Primal and dual problems. Sensitivity analysis. Integer programming and the Branch and Bound Method. Solving the transportation problem using MODI. The assignment problem. Binary programming and the Balas Method. Network problems. Dynamic programming.
learning_outcomes: To apply basic linear algebra to solve linear programming problems. To generate different solutions to a problem and to compare them to the optimal one. To develop mathematical models for a large variety of systems. In class discussion helps to analyze and develop decision models. To learn how to recognize and solve problems with constraints. To learn how to use computer packages to solve linear programming problems.
literature: Investigación de Operaciones (10a ed.). Hillier, Frederick y Lieberman, Gerald. McGraw-Hill, 2015. Operations Research - An Introduction”. (9th ed.). Taha, Hamdy A. Prentice Hall, 2011. Operations Research Applications and Algorithms, (3rd ed.) Winston W. Prentice Hall Hispanoamericana Duxbury Press, 2004.

aims: Principles and training required to develop a project using computer simulation as an analytical tool. Fundamentals of discrete event simulation, generation of random variables, input analysis, output analysis and training in the use of high level software to design, implement and validate a simulation model.
course_content: Review of probability and stochastic processes. Building simulation models using a spreadsheet. Random number generation. Random variate generation. Input modeling. Output analysis. Building simulation models using commercial simulation software.
learning_outcomes: Develop algorithms to generate uniform and non-uniform random-variates. Simulate a non-stationary Poisson process. Build a simulation model using a spreadsheet. Use a software to select an input probability distribution. Compute and interpret a half-width from the output of a terminating simulation. Compute and interpret a half-width from the output of a terminating simulation. Perform a test of paired data to compare alternative system designs. Build a simulation model using commercial simulation software.
literature: Simulation (5th ed.). S. M. Ross. Prentice Hall, 2010. Simio & Simulation: Modeling, Analysis and Applications (3rd ed.). W. D. Kelton, J. S. Smith, D. T. Sturrock, 2014. Introducción a la Ingeniería (2nd ed.), S. Romero, O. Romero, D. F. Muñoz. Cengage Learning, 2015

aims: Concepts and modern techniques required for an effective and efficient management of projects, from its conceptual and planning phases to its termination.
course_content: Introduction to project management and evaluation Strategic management and project selection Management Risk Project planning Budget estimation Project scheduling Resource management Project evaluation and control Project closure
learning_outcomes: Identification of activities required to performed a project Solve problems dealing with resource constraints Use software for project management Prepare a project charter Risk Management Monitoring and control of project
